architecture.summary: The paper investigates LoRA-induced representational changes in Gemma-2-9B by training adapter-specific Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) on delta activations (residual stream contributions from LoRA adapters alone) across multiple transformer layers. Information flows through the model's residual stream; LoRA updates are isolated via delta activation subtraction, then decomposed by SAEs trained on this adapter-specific signal. Comparison with pretrained SAE dictionaries uses cosine similarity of decoder directions, principal-angle analysis, and Centered Kernel Alignment (CKA) to measure geometric alignment between learned and pretrained feature spaces.
trained_components: adapter-specific SAEs (one per layer and rank combination)

biblio.title: Feature Geometry of LoRA Adapters: A Sparse Autoencoder Analysis of Representational Divergence in Fine-Tuned Language Models

claims.statement: Across layers and ranks, we consistently observe comparatively weak geometric alignment between LoRA-induced feature dictionaries and pretrained SAE features.

Adapter-specific SAEs reconstruct delta activations more effectively than pretrained SAEs, suggesting that LoRA updates occupy partially distinct representational structure within the residual stream.
Feature density increases with rank and depth, while geometric divergence remains relatively stable across ranks.
LoRA fine-tuning can induce feature structures that are not fully captured by pretrained interpretability dictionaries.
